story:
You would never find her unless someone whispered the way. After nightfall I followed the embroidery group’s directions past the bakery, down the alley with the unlit lamppost, and into a dim hallway at the bottom of a mossy stair. Behind weathered green French doors a bell chimed and a woman said, Welcome.

On the posters she is monstrous; in person she is the kind of beauty you cannot look away from—porcelain face, dark crown of hair, eyes like a hungry tiger. Her wind-chime voice offered tea and a seat. The room was shabby: faded clippings on the walls, a tattered blue curtain, shelves of books and bottles people call witchcraft. The only fine thing was a heavy gold watch on her wrist. She caught me staring and said it had belonged to her first husband. One day she would melt it into a necklace. She laughed, and I felt unease and curiosity braid together.

She spoke of her past. She had once been a leading lady men crossed the city to see. Desire made them sloppy and cruel; one rich man took what he wanted and then made it legal. He called himself husband. When no child came, I turned ugly to him, she said, and he acted ugly accordingly. One night he threatened to smear my name and leave. I told him that as long as I lived, I would never bring a child into his reach. He tried to make my words disappear in flames. The scar along her jaw glinted like a ring of lightning. Neither of us mentioned my own bruise hidden by a high neckline.

She did not know then she was with child. After, no one would ever get to know. She made a small marker though there was nothing to bury. A month later the man was in the ground beside it, claimed quickly by a disease—got him like poison, essentially. Whether fate or intention, she let people think what they wanted. That was when her “charity work” began, she said with a thin smile: a fierce sort of protection for women no one else protects. She did not glorify it; she spoke with the coolness of someone who lived through fire and decided the flames would not touch her again.

Now, love. How would you like your tragedy to occur?

Widows choose differently, she said; worse men earn worse ends, though there is not always rhyme to it. I thought of the French woman my husband kept, of my children, of the fading and returning bruises. In two weeks, I said, he will be drunk, hopeless, and the river will take him from the bridge. People will not speak of such a death. We will wear black the proper length of time. Our black-and-blue will fade. I will take my children to the countryside. I hope I will not regret it. She told me she had not once regretted anything. I believed her and did not let it make me cruel.

When I rose to leave, she took my hand—cool, unassuming porcelain. The posters call her grotesque and count three men, which tells me the posters know nothing. We women must stick together, she murmured. I thought of my daughter and vowed she would never need directions to this door.

A faint sound threaded the air. She drew back the curtain. A cage of green bodies writhed and hissed, a single trembling creature made of many. Don’t worry, darling, she said, eyes briefly bright. Their venom is reserved for the venomous.
